title: Batch Processing: Managed Batch with Warm Worker Pool
description: Batch job execution using managed batch services (AWS Batch with warm pools, Azure Batch, Google Batch) with pre-provisioned container instances maintained in a warm standby state. Achieves P95 job start times of 10-60 seconds by keeping a pool of ready-to-execute workers alive, eliminating container pull and VM provisioning delays. Balances job start latency and infrastructure cost — idle warm workers incur standby costs but eliminate cold-start delays. Best for workloads with predictable load patterns, sub-minute start requirements, and batch jobs running multiple times per hour.

cost.costNotes: Warm pool costs include standby infrastructure charges even when no jobs are running. Min ($150): small warm pool (2-4 instances t3.medium in standby ~$50/mo) + compute for jobs + storage. Max ($6000): large warm pools for high-frequency jobs with significant data processing.

provenance.estimatedMonthlyRangeUsd: Warm pool batch adds idle standby costs vs cold-start. Min ($150): 2x t3.medium warm instances (24/7 standby ~$50/mo) + periodic batch compute ($70-80/mo) + S3 storage ($20). Max ($6000): 10x c5.2xlarge warm pool ($800/mo idle) + production batch compute at peak ($3500-4000/mo) + data transfer and storage ($1000+).
provenance.adoptionCost: Warm pool configuration adds complexity over base batch: pool sizing, idle timeout tuning, health monitoring for warm workers, and cost optimization for standby instances. Estimate 13-14 developer days at ~$180/day = $2500.
provenance.licenseCost: No license cost. AWS Batch warm pools, Azure Batch, and Google Batch are usage-based managed services with no upfront licensing.

provides.reasoning: Provides batch processing capability via managed batch services with warm workers — processing data in scheduled or event-driven batches with fast job start.
Enables ETL batch workflows with fast job start suited to sub-hourly batch pipelines.
Warm worker pools enable high throughput through pre-provisioned parallelism, avoiding scheduling overhead of cold-start batch.

requires.reasoning: Managed batch services provide built-in schedulers; external schedulers (EventBridge, Airflow) can trigger jobs.
Orchestration required to manage job submission, monitor warm pool health, and coordinate multi-step batch workflows.
Batch jobs require persistent storage for input data and results; warm workers maintain storage connections between jobs.
Managed batch services provide retry capabilities; warm pool configuration must handle worker failures without draining the pool.

supports_nfr.reason: Managed batch with warm pools is compatible with any auth mechanism; job-level auth is handled by IAM roles attached to worker instances.
Managed batch services provide job execution logs and CloudTrail/Activity Log integration; compatible with both audited and non-audited environments.
Warm pool batch supports tenant isolation through separate job queues per tenant with IAM-scoped worker roles.
Managed batch services with warm pools can achieve up to 99.99% availability; five-nine availability requires active-active multi-region topology.
Warm pool batch is optimized for 10-60s P95 job start. Tolerating >5 minute start times suggests cold-start batch is sufficient and more cost-effective.

Managed batch with warm worker pools achieves P95 job start in 10-60 seconds. Workers remain in warm standby but require job dispatch, container allocation from pool, and setup. Cannot guarantee sub-10s consistently at P95.
P99 job start for warm pool batch is 60-180 seconds, accounting for pool exhaustion events requiring new worker provisioning under high concurrency.
Warm pool batch RTO is 10-30 minutes: warm workers restart quickly but failed jobs must be detected, re-queued, and re-assigned to an available worker.
RPO of 5+ minutes for warm pool batch; data arriving between batch runs is at risk until re-processing from last checkpoint.
Warm pool batch handles up to 360M jobs/hour with sufficient pool sizing and horizontal scaling of managed batch services.
Warm pool batch maintains idle workers between jobs, incurring standby costs. At <4 jobs/hour (1 job per 15+ minutes), cold-start batch is more cost-effective.
Managed batch with warm pools is compatible with PII workloads when workers are configured with appropriate encryption and access controls.
Warm pool batch is compatible with GDPR through proper data handling and deletion capabilities within batch jobs.
Warm pool batch supports HIPAA compliance with encryption at rest/transit and appropriate BAA coverage for managed services.
Warm pool batch is compatible with CCPA when batch jobs support consumer data deletion within required timeframes.
Warm pool batch supports SOX compliance through job execution logs and managed service audit trails.
Warm pool batch is inherently eventually consistent. Read-your-writes requires compensating patterns (polling, outbox) on top.
Strict durability achievable with idempotent jobs and checkpointing to durable storage; warm pool state is ephemeral.

supports_constraints.reason: Warm pool batch is available on all major clouds (AWS Batch warm pools, Azure Batch, Google Batch) and on-prem via Kubernetes Job pools.
Managed batch services support all languages via containerized job images.
Warm pool batch targets scheduled job execution platforms; api platform excluded as batch is not a request-response architecture.
Warm pool batch works with or without caching; warm workers can maintain connection pools to caches between jobs.
Warm pool batch can use message queues for job dispatch but primarily relies on managed batch job queues.
Warm pool batch supports AI inference with pre-loaded models in warm workers, suitable for batch embedding generation and bulk predictions.
Warm pool batch supports multi-tenant workloads through separate job queues with namespace-isolated warm pools per tenant.
Pattern requires batch_processing feature flag — this is the warm-pool variant of the batch processing implementation pattern.
Warm pool batch can coordinate distributed transactions within job execution but requires saga patterns for multi-step transactions.
Warm pool batch is compatible with lambda architecture combining managed batch and real-time streaming.
Warm pool batch can build vector indexes and generate embeddings with models pre-loaded in warm workers.
Warm pool batch works with document stores; warm workers maintain persistent connections between jobs.
Warm pool batch can use key-value stores for job state; warm workers benefit from persistent connection pooling.
Warm pool batch supports graph analytics and bulk graph mutations in scheduled batch jobs.
Warm pool batch handles time-series aggregation and historical rollup jobs with predictable scheduling.
Warm pool batch can execute bulk OLTP operations during off-peak windows with fast job start.
Warm pool batch naturally supports OLAP aggregation with predictable sub-minute start times.

worker_type.description: Execution environment. Warm-pool: managed batch service (AWS Batch with warm pools, Azure Batch, Google Batch) with pre-provisioned worker instances in standby state.

schedule_type.description: How batches are triggered. Cron is most common for warm pool batch with predictable execution windows.

retry_strategy.description: How failed jobs are retried. Exponential_backoff recommended to avoid pool resource contention.

parallelism.description: Execution model. Partitioned recommended: split input across multiple workers in the warm pool for parallel processing.

failure_handling.description: What happens when individual records fail. Dead_letter_queue recommended for production warm pool batch to capture failures without blocking the pool.

warn_nfr.reason: Warm-pool batch with availability target <95% indicates non-critical workload; simpler cron-based solutions may be more cost-effective than managed batch infrastructure.
warn_nfr.message: data-batch-processing--warm-pool: availability target is {actual} (<95%). For non-critical batch jobs, simpler cron-based execution may reduce operational overhead.
